Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

(Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise) (1) (The reality of Tasbih and its various aspects): The meaning of the word 'Tasbih' (glorification) has been explained in several places. It contains the concept of declaring Allah free from all imperfections (Tanzeeh) as well as the concept of servitude and worship. This expression occurs through both word and deed. Declaring Allah's purity with the tongue is Tasbih, and performing prayer—including standing, bowing, and prostrating before Him—is also Tasbih. Its forms vary. Every entity in the universe glorifies its Lord in one way or another. The Quran even highlights the reality that those humans who do not glorify Him willingly (taw'an) must do so unwillingly (karhan) within the cosmic framework of creation. Consequently, people are urged not to create disharmony between their sphere of free will and their cosmic sphere of existence. This point has been elaborated in its respective place. Wherever the Quran refers to the glorification of all things in the universe, it generally serves three objectives:

First, to remind humanity of this truth: when every entity in this universe, large or small, recognizes the right of its Creator and Owner and glorifies Him, then this duty lies upon human beings to an even greater degree. This is because Allah blessed humanity with the status of being the noblest of creations and bestowed upon them the mantle of His vicegerency. Second, to encourage the believers to fulfill this duty so that they do not lose heart or courage upon seeing the abundance of ungrateful people in the world. If those who recognize their Lord's right among humans are few, there is no need to be disheartened. The rest of the entire universe is actively engaged in glorifying and sanctifying its Lord. The traveler on this path is not alone; rather, this is a path crowded with caravans.

Third, to express detachment from those who, despite being reminded, evade the worship of Allah, and to let them know that if they do not glorify Him, He is not in need of their glorification. Every single thing in this universe glorifies Him; if some unfortunate humans turn away, they do not harm Allah in the least, but only harm themselves. In some places, the Quran keeps all three of these objectives in view. In other places, one or two of them are intended. Distinguishing between them is not particularly difficult if one maintains focus on the structural coherence of the text.

(Demands of the attributes Mighty and Wise): (And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise) means that since Allah is Mighty and simultaneously Wise, He alone is entitled to have everyone glorify and worship Him. 'Al-Aziz' (the Mighty) means the One Who overcomes all things, possesses all authority, and no one is beyond His reach or can overpower Him or influence Him through force. Alongside this, He is 'Al-Hakim' (the Wise), which is why every act of His is based on wisdom, justice, and mercy. It is not that He does whatever He wants merely because of His power, without any wisdom or purpose. This universe is a small manifestation of His power, and its very existence bears witness that its Creator dominates all things and that profound wisdom is hidden in all His works. This wisdom of His demands that He bring about a Day in which He rewards those who spent their lives within His designated boundaries, and punishes those who transgressed those boundaries. If He were unable to do so, it would contradict His honor and power; and if He were able but chose not to do so, it would contradict His wisdom, justice, and mercy. Without this, this world would be reduced to a playground for children and a place of lawlessness, and it is entirely against the dignity of a Mighty and Wise being to create such a vast system merely for sport.

(His is the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and causes death, and He is Able to do all things) (2)

(Reference to further attributes): This means that the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth is in His hands alone; no one else shares in His authority to interfere in any manner. He alone gives life and causes death. When life is granted by Him and death occurs by His command, there is no basis for anyone else to share in His kingdom. (And He is Able to do all things) means that no task is beyond His power such that He would require an assistant or helper. He directly controls every inch of His kingdom and can bring about whatever He wills directly through His word of command: 'Kun' (Be).

(He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He is All-Knower of everything) (3) After stating the encompassment of His power, this verse explains the encompassment of His knowledge: He is the First and He is the Last. When nothing existed, He was there; and when nothing remains, He will still be there. He initiated everything, and ultimately the inheritance of everything returns to Him. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) explained (the Manifest and the Hidden) as: 'You are the Manifest, so there is nothing above You; and You are the Hidden, so there is nothing beyond You.' In verse 13, these words are used in the same sense as we use 'inside and outside,' meaning that Allah's knowledge encompasses the inner and outer aspects of everything. (And He is All-Knower of everything) is presented as a universal summary: He knows everything, and the manifest and hidden are entirely equal to Him.

(He it is Who created the heavens and the earth in six days and then established Himself on the Throne. He knows what goes into the earth and what comes out of it, and what descends from the heaven and what ascends into it. And He is with you wherever you may be. And Allah is the All-Seer of what you do) (4) This is a further elaboration of the encompassment of His knowledge and power: Allah is the One Who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and after creating them, He did not leave their management to others and retire to a corner. Instead, He Himself is established upon the Throne of authority, overseeing the entire administration. The 'Throne' represents the reins of power and sovereignty. We have explained 'in six days' in other places: it does not refer to our human days, but to divine epochs.

(The meticulous design of the universe proves its purposeful nature): Wherever the creation of the heavens and the earth in six days is mentioned in the Quran, the purpose is to draw our attention to the special care Allah observed in creating them. From this, the conclusion is drawn: how is it possible that after creating this universe with such care, He would remain entirely detached from it? That He would have no concern with its good, nor any relation to its evil, allowing people to cause whatever chaos they wish while He looks on like a silent spectator? If He were to do so, it would mean He created this entire universe merely as a plaything—exerting great care in its creation, yet without any ultimate purpose. (He knows what goes into the earth and what comes out of it, and what descends from the heaven and what ascends into it. And He is with you wherever you may be. And Allah is the All-Seer of what you do) explains that He oversees His kingdom with absolute vigilance and attentiveness. It is stated that He knows whatever enters the earth and whatever emerges from it. Similarly, whatever descends from the sky is in His knowledge, and whatever ascends to it is known to Him, and He is with you wherever you are, seeing all that you do.

(His is the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned) (5)

This means that Allah is not only aware of everything, but practically all matters return to Him. All affairs proceed by His command, and then the report of all of them is presented before Him. His agents refer to Him for all instructions and present their performance in His presence. No one is authorized to act purely on their own discretion, nor is anyone exempt from accountability before Him.

(He merges the night into the day and merges the day into the night, and He is the All-Knower of what is in the breasts) (6) This cycle of night and day does not run on its own, nor does anyone else run it; rather, it is Allah Who operates it. He is the One Who enters the night into the day and the day into the night, and they pursue each other in constant, active succession.

(And He is the All-Knower of what is in the breasts): Since He is the One Who brings both the light of day and the darkness of night—manifesting the light of day to dispel the universal darkness of night and unveiling everything covered by it—how can anything remain hidden from Him? He is fully aware of the most hidden secrets, even the thoughts concealed within the breasts.

2. Subject Matter of Verses 7–15

Next, the verses address those Muslims who, though they entered the fold of Islam by uttering the declaration of faith and pledging 'we hear and we obey,' proved weak in meeting the demands of faith when the stage of spending (Infaq) and striving (Jihaad) arrived. These weak Muslims are warned to evaluate their character in the mirror of Allah's attributes and to fulfill the demands of the faith they have claimed. Today, if they wish, they can secure their position among the early pioneers of Islam by responding to the Messenger's call to spend and strive; otherwise, they must remember that their end will be with the hypocrites, and the opportunity they lose today will never be regained.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

(Believe in Allah and His Messenger, and spend of that whereof He has made you trustees. And such of you as believe and spend, theirs will be a great reward) (7). (General address warning the hypocrites): Although the command 'Believe' (Aminu) appears general, the context reveals that the address is actually directed at those weak in faith and the hypocrites who claimed belief in Allah and His Messenger, but shied away and avoided doing so when the practical demands of faith appeared in the form of spending and striving. Addressing these claimants of faith, He commanded them to believe. Obviously, the verb 'Aminu' is used here in its true and complete sense, just as in the verse: 'O you who believe, believe!' (meaning: O you who claim faith, bring about a true and firm faith). Examples of a verb being used first in its basic sense and then in its complete sense have been discussed previously.

(An essential demand of faith): (And spend of that whereof He has made you trustees) states both the essential demand of this faith and its clear rational and natural evidence. This means that the right of faith in Allah and His Messenger is not fulfilled merely by declaring 'we believe in Allah and His Messenger,' but requires that when a call is made to sacrifice wealth and life in the way of Allah, one must respond. How can those who do not even have the heart to spend wealth be expected to sacrifice their lives? And if they have the heart for neither, what value does their claim to faith hold?

(Wealth is a trust from Allah): (Of that whereof He has made you trustees) means that regarding this wealth, you must keep this reality in mind: you are neither its creator nor its absolute owner; rather, Allah has made you trustees over it so that you may utilize it within His prescribed limits and remain prepared to account for every single penny before Him. The point is that since you are not the creator and owner of this wealth but merely its trustees, what is the meaning of behaving miserly when the demand to spend this wealth comes from the One Who made you trustees over it?

(Spending confirms faith): (And such of you as believe and spend, theirs will be a great reward)—here, 'and spend' (anfiqu) appears as a confirmation and testimony of faith. That is, those who, after expressing faith, confirm their declaration through spending can rest assured that this transaction is not a losing bargain. Rather, there is a very great reward for them with Allah, where they will receive tenfold or more. Why then should they hesitate to invest capital in such a highly profitable business?

Recall what was mentioned above: the divine attributes described in the introduction of the Surah serve as a mirror, so that those who claim faith may reform their morals and conduct. It was stated earlier that Allah is the First and the Last, and to Him all things return. Obviously, when this is the absolute reality, whatever man has acquired in this world does not belong to him; he is merely a trustee on behalf of Allah. Since the original Owner is Allah, what is the sense in withholding His wealth from Him!

(And what is the matter with you that you believe not in Allah, while the Messenger invites you to believe in your Lord and He has indeed taken your covenant, if you are real believers?) (8) This is a form of reproachful admonition to these weak Muslims: when you have already made a covenant of hearing and obeying with the Messenger, the demand of this covenant is that you act upon whatever command is revealed by Allah and whatever the Messenger invites you to, saying 'we believe and we confirm.' Yet your state is such that the Messenger invites you to spend in Allah's way, and you turn away! What kind of covenant and what sort of faith is this? If this is the state of your faith and covenant today while the Messenger is present among you and personally inviting you to fulfill the demands of faith, what will be your state tomorrow when the Messenger is no longer among you? Two points emerge clearly from this verse. First, the essential demand of faith is to believe in everything revealed by Allah and invited to by the Messenger; rejecting even one of these things is equivalent to rejecting all of them. Second, faith takes shape and exists through action, and spending has a special role in its growth, nourishment, and strength; hence, the invitation to spend is phrased here as an invitation to faith.

(The covenant refers to the pledge of hearing and obeying): In my view, the 'covenant' (Meethaq) here refers to the pledge of hearing and obeying (Sam' wa Ta'ah) that every Muslim had to make when swearing allegiance to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). My esteemed teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) seemed inclined toward the covenant mentioned in Surah Al-Imran, verse 80. I have reflected on this, but it does not fit the context here; that belongs to a different context. My view is supported by Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 7: 'And remember Allah's favor upon you and His covenant which He bound you with when you said, "We hear and we obey."'

(He it is Who sends down manifest verses to His slave that He may bring you out from darkness into light. And verily, Allah is to you Full of Kindness, Most Merciful) (9).

(Spending brings one out of darkness into light): This means you should not flee from the Messenger's invitation to spend, viewing it as a burden or harmful to you. Allah is revealing these clear verses to His Messenger so that He may bring you out of the darkness of personal desires and love for the world, into the light of faith and love for the Hereafter. Do not assume that Allah wants to subject you to losses and hardships. He is Kind and Merciful; He is opening the path for your well-being in both this world and the next, rather than placing you in difficulty. (Manifest verses) refers generally to all the teachings that people were receiving through the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and here it points particularly to the verses concerning spending and striving, a portion of which appears in this Surah with clear arguments. (Darkness) refers to the darkness of physical desires and love of this world—for which the sole cure is spending in the way of Allah—while (Light) refers to the light generated by spending, which is mentioned later in verse 21. 'Ra'oof' (Kind) and 'Raheem' (Merciful) have been explained in their respective places. In the first attribute, the aspect of averting harm is dominant; in the second, the aspect of establishing good is dominant.

(And what is the matter with you that you spend not in the way of Allah? And to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth. Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquering and fought. Such are higher in degree than those who spent afterwards and fought. But to all, Allah has promised the best. And Allah is All-Knower of what you do) (10)

(Two arguments encouraging spending): This encourages those weak Muslims to spend, presenting two motivating arguments. The first is what was indicated above: Allah is the Owner of all things in the heavens and the earth. In this world, whatever control man possesses over things is entirely temporary, held in the capacity of a trustee and vicegerent of Allah. Ultimately, everything returns to Him. Since man's status is merely that of a temporary trustee, what is the meaning of sitting upon the entrusted wealth as if one were its absolute owner? The correct attitude is that even if he is miserly with himself, he should give generously when requested by the One Who placed the trust in his care.

The second is that changes in circumstances greatly affect the value of deeds. Today, when disbelief and its adherents dominate Makkah (note that this Surah was revealed before the Conquest of Makkah) and the dread of Quraysh still prevails over Arabia, those who spend their wealth to make Islam victorious and participate in fighting will hold a far higher rank with Allah than those who spend and strive after the Conquest of Makkah, when the power of Quraysh is broken. Most fortunate are those who find the opportunity to serve Islam during today's difficult conditions. The point is: do not lose this opportunity through lack of courage, but strive to secure your place among the early pioneers of Islam and those close to Allah.

It was explained in Surah Al-Waqi'ah that the majority of those in the ranks of the pioneers and those near to Allah will be those who served the faith with determination during the early period of Islam's vulnerability. Only a few from later generations will find a place in their ranks, and those will be people who passed through severe trials and made great sacrifices for the faith.

(But to all, Allah has promised the best. And Allah is All-Knower of what you do) means that Allah's promise to both groups is good. Those who sacrifice for Islam after the Conquest of Makkah also have ranks and positions according to their services. All who serve Islam with good intention and sincerity will receive the reward for their sacrifices; indeed, some among them may even join the ranks of the early pioneers. Nevertheless, the period before the Conquest of Makkah is of a different order. Blessed are those who recognize the value of that period and benefit from it.

(The Conquest refers to the Conquest of Makkah): Here, 'the Conquest' (Al-Fath) clearly refers to the Conquest of Makkah. Some have interpreted it as the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, but Hudaybiyyah was not the actual conquest itself; rather, it was the prelude to the Conquest of Makkah. The word 'Conquest' naturally directs the mind to the Conquest of Makkah. During this struggle, it was firmly established in the heart of every Muslim as a matter of belief that the ultimate objective of the mission of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was to purify the House of Allah from all defilements of disbelief and polytheism, presenting it in its original Abrahamic beauty. Thus, every true Muslim longed and waited for this event, while only the hypocrites remained hesitant due to their weak resolve. Their hesitation is mentioned later as proof of their hypocrisy.

(Who is he that will lend to Allah a goodly loan, then He will increase it manifold to his credit, and he will have a honorable reward?) (11)

(A highly effective appeal for financial support for Jihad): This is an appeal in clear and moving terms for financial contribution toward Jihad: who is there to offer Allah a goodly loan, so that He may multiply it for him and grant him an honorable reward? Although the word 'loan' (Qard) can refer to spending in general, the context makes it clear that here it specifically means financial support for Jihad. We will point out some contextual clues to this effect later, God willing.

An appeal for spending in the way of Allah using the word 'loan' requires no elaboration. It has been made clear above that whatever man possesses is an entrusted deposit from Allah. Now, how great is the favor of this Generous Lord that when He invites His servants to spend His own bestowed wealth in His way, He terms it a loan upon Himself, for the repayment of which He is responsible, just as a debtor is responsible for returning a sum to a creditor. (Then He will increase it manifold) means that Allah is not asking for this loan because of any shortage in His treasuries. When everything is created and granted by Him, how could there be any question of shortage? Rather, He asks for it only to deposit His servants' given wealth in His bank and multiply it, so that its profit may serve them in an eternal life as an everlasting capital. The critique of usurers implied in this style of expression is not hidden from those of fine taste. The point is that whoever accumulates capital in the banks of this world finds that this world and its capital are temporary, whereas those who deposit their wealth with their Lord find their profit eternal and safe from all risks. Most translators have rendered 'Mudha'afah' as 'doubling,' but this is not entirely accurate; it means increasing or multiplying. This multiplication can be double, fourfold, tenfold, or even far more. It depends on the sincerity of the giver, the circumstances in which the wealth was given, and, above all, the grace of the Generous Lord Who has opened the path of eternal benefit for His servants.

(Conditions of a goodly loan): Only one condition is attached here: that this loan be a 'goodly loan' (Qard Hasan). The explanations provided in the Quran show that a loan becomes 'goodly' when it is given with complete generosity and high resolve, not with a tight heart, reluctantly, or for show; when it is given from good wealth, not merely as a way to get rid of low-quality goods; and, above all, when it is given solely for the pleasure of Allah, not for any worldly purpose, and without following it up with reminders of generosity or causing hurt. (And he will have an honorable reward) means that the multiplication of the spent wealth will occur, and in addition, Allah will grant him a highly honorable reward out of His special grace, as explained below.

(On the Day you shall see the believing men and the believing women: their light running forward before them and by their right hands. Glad tidings for you this Day! Gardens under which rivers flow, to dwell therein forever! Truly, this is the great success) (12)

(Spending will become light on the Day of Resurrection): This explains a specific aspect of that honorable reward. It means that Allah will guide those who spend in His way toward Paradise on the Day of Gathering by causing their light to run before them and on their right, and they will proceed toward Paradise in this light, while others who did not generate this light within themselves will be enveloped in darkness. Looking at the context of the verses, this light will be acquired through the blessing of that very spending to which they are being invited here. We indicated under verse 9 ('He it is Who sends down manifest verses to His slave that He may bring you out from darkness into light') that 'manifest verses' refers particularly to those verses revealed to clarify the greatness and importance of spending. It is this spending that cuts the root of hypocrisy and bestows the light of wisdom that guides man through the darkness of this world and will guide him in the Hereafter as well. 'Sa'y' (running) here does not mean jogging; this word is also used to describe doing a task with eagerness and diligence, which is its dominant usage here. This light will spread only in front of them and on their right; its reflection will not fall on their left, so that the people of the left (Ashaab al-Shimaal) cannot benefit from it. Their deprivation of this light is mentioned next.

(Glad tidings for you this Day! Gardens under which rivers flow, to dwell therein forever! Truly, this is the great success): This glad tidings can be a description of the state of affairs or spoken by the angels; Quranic precedents support both views. (Truly, this is the great success) means that if Allah has granted wealth to someone, the greatest benefit they can derive from it is to spend it in His way to generate the light within their heart that will guide them through the darkness of the Day of Judgment. All other benefits are temporary and fleeting, and the harm latent within them is permanent and eternal. The point is: if you have the courage, spend your wealth to achieve this great success.

(On the Day when the hypocritical men and hypocritical women will say to those who believe: "Wait for us! Let us get something from your light!" It will be said: "Go back to your rear! Then seek a light!" So a wall will be put up between them, with a gate therein. Inside it will be mercy, and outside it will be torment) (13)

(Deprivation of the hypocrites from light): On that Day, the state of the hypocrites will be that they wander in darkness. They did not try to generate that light within themselves by spending in Allah's way, which would have benefited them in the Hereafter. When they see a group of those with whom they lived in the world possessing a light that guides them in this deep darkness, they will request them with great regret: 'Please let us come close to you so that we too may benefit from your light.' The reply will be: 'Do not hope to benefit from this light. Go back and try to find light there, if you can.' This means that the place to acquire this light was left behind in the worldly life; the time to obtain it has passed. For you, there is nothing here now but regret, remorse, and this darkness. Some have unfortunately interpreted this reply as a mere deception or trick, but it is not a trick; it is a statement of reality. The place of acquisition and earning is this world. Whoever did not earn goodness in it will have nothing but deprivation in the Hereafter. Immediately after this response, a wall will be placed between them and the believers, containing only one gate. Inside this gate will be mercy, and outside it will be punishment. The believers will enter the portion of mercy through this gate, and the hypocrites will remain enveloped in the darkness of punishment. A similar wall is mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf; refer there for further details.

((The hypocrites) will call the believers: "Were we not with you?" The believers will reply: "Yes! But you led yourselves into temptations, you looked forward for our destruction, you doubted, and you were deceived by false desires, till the Command of Allah came. And the chief deceiver deceived you in respect of Allah") (14)

(The plea of the hypocrites and the response to them): When the hypocrites see that even the glimpse of light they saw has vanished and their connection with those they lived with in the world is entirely broken, they will cry out in heartbreak: 'O brothers, you have completely cut us off! Did we not live with you in the world? Why this indifference and neglect toward your brothers?!' The reply will be: 'There is no doubt that outwardly you were with us in the world, but your hearts were not with us. Instead, you remained involved in those very trials from which Allah wished to extract you. You claimed faith with loud voices, but when its demands came before you, you did not have the courage to fulfill any of them. You held your wealth and your lives dearer than Allah's religion and His Messenger; your loyalties remained with the enemies of Islam, and you always preferred your personal interests over the truth.'

(And you looked forward for our destruction): This means you constantly waited for misfortunes and disasters to befall us. In Surah At-Tawbah, the character of these hypocrites is described in these words: 'And among the bedouins are some who look upon what they spend in Allah's way as a fine and watch for disasters to suffer you' (Surah At-Tawbah: 98). The character of these same people is described in Surah An-Nisa: 'Those who wait and watch about you; if you gain a victory from Allah, they say: "Were we not with you?"' (Surah An-Nisa: 141).

(And you doubted): This means you remained in doubt and hesitation. Your hearts were never firm on the truth of Islam and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). You wished to maintain some connection with both disbelief and Islam, planning to align your future with whichever side emerged victorious. This mental state is depicted elsewhere as: 'Swaying between this and that, belonging neither to these nor to those' (Surah An-Nisa: 143).

(And you were deceived by false desires, till the Command of Allah came. And the chief deceiver deceived you in respect of Allah): This means you remained trapped in false hopes. The respite granted to you by Allah misled you into thinking that your double-dealing policy was successful and that you could carry it through to the end, but this hope could not be fulfilled. Satan kept you in deception, and Allah's decree to make the truth dominant was issued, forcing you to see this evil day. In the phrase 'And the chief deceiver deceived you,' the word 'Gharoor' refers to Satan. Deceiving them in respect of Allah means that while they were warned that Allah had sent His Messenger to establish final proof, and that those who persisted in disbelief or tried to hide behind hypocrisy would inevitably be seized by Him, Satan taught them that these were merely empty threats that were never true in the past and would not be in the future, so there was no need to worry.

(So this Day no ransom shall be taken from you, nor of those who disbelieved. Your abode is the Fire, that is your proper place, and worst indeed is that destination) (15)

This means that the situation you face has come after complete warning and the establishment of proof; therefore, there is no way to escape it now. No one here has anything to offer as ransom, nor will ransom be accepted from you or from those disbelievers with whom you held close relations. Since you did not align yourself fully with Islam, your end will be alongside these disbelievers despite your claim to faith. The Fire is the destination for all of you, and it is your 'Mawla'—meaning your return and proper place. The point is that there is no room for you to plead before Allah, nor will you be heard; your return is to the Fire. Whatever you receive will be from it, and remember that it is an evil destination.

4. Subject Matter of Verses 16–24

The subsequent verses continue to address those hypocrites discussed in the preceding section. They are first warned of the consequences of their hesitant attitude: if their hesitation is not resolved even after seeing such clear signs in support of the Messenger (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), their hearts will eventually suffer the same hardness that befell the Jews, leading Allah to seal their hearts. Afterward, they are encouraged not to fear current circumstances and to trust in Allah's power and mercy. The God Who revives the dead earth is fully capable of granting such success to this call of truth that no trace of disbelief remains. In this context, they are also reassured that those who spend in Allah's way will not suffer loss but will receive many times over, and this is the path to entering the ranks of the truthful (Siddiqeen) and the witnesses (Shuhada) of this Ummah.

Then, a picture is drawn of the transience of the worldly things for the love of which people have forgotten the eternal kingdom—which is as vast as the heavens and the earth—and which they could acquire in exchange for the worthless worldly items they accumulate. Finally, guidance is provided on the point that ease and hardship, joy and sorrow, poverty and wealth in this world do not depend on human planning alone, but on the divine decree established by Allah. Therefore, it is not permissible for a human being to become arrogant when receiving abundance, attributing it to their own planning, nor is it permissible to lose heart and lament when facing a trial. Instead, one should adopt gratitude in blessings and patience in adversity, keeping the heart anchored to the Lord in both states. With this perspective, recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

(Has not the time come for the hearts of those who believe to be affected by Allah's Reminder and that which has been revealed of the truth, lest they become as those who received the Scripture before, and the term was prolonged for them so their hearts were hardened? And many of them were rebellious) (16). (Clarifying the demands of faith to the hypocrites): As indicated, 'those who believe' (Lilladhina amanu) refers to the hypocrites whose attitude is under discussion. Since they claimed faith, they are referred to by this description so that they might reflect seriously on the demands of faith and fulfill them if they possess any sincerity toward their claim. The phrasing 'Has not the time come' (Alam ya'ni) indicates that this Surah was revealed at a time when so many proofs of the truth and dominance of the faith had appeared that any remaining doubts should have vanished. If their doubts persisted after seeing all these signs, it meant they were not prepared to accept Islam with an open heart until they saw every claim fulfilled before their eyes. Such faith is not acceptable to Allah; hence, they are warned that if they continue in doubt, their state will become like that of the Jews, who remained in doubt until a long period passed over them and their hearts hardened, losing all capacity for spiritual growth, leading Allah to seal them, as detailed in Surah Al-Baqarah.

(A Divine Law): This clarifies a reality: the arising of doubt or hesitation regarding a matter of truth is not surprising, nor is its persistence for a time entirely blameworthy. This state can occur even in a well-intentioned person. However, if a person uses these doubts as a shield to suppress the voice of truth within themselves and as an excuse to cling to falsehood, then, according to the Divine Law, such a person is deprived of the capacity to accept the truth. Allah does not tolerate the disregard of truth for long.

(The disease of doubt is fatal): In the phrase (to be affected by Allah's Reminder and that which has been revealed of the truth), 'Allah's Reminder' (Zikrullah) and 'the truth' (Al-Haqq) refer to the Holy Quran itself, but two different terms are used to highlight two distinct aspects. 'Allah's Reminder' refers to the warnings concerning the dangers and destructions that will inevitably face those who seek excuses to turn away from the truth, while 'the truth' refers to the universal principles that the Quran has highlighted anew, separating them from falsehood. It is stated that so many proofs have appeared in their support that people's hearts should have humbled themselves before them. If they have not done so, it means they are suffering from the same disease that afflicted the Jews.

It has been explained in detail under various Surahs that the Jews were so diseased with doubt that even in the presence of their Prophet, and while witnessing miracles and signs of Allah's power at every step, they remained in constant uncertainty. This illness eventually deprived them of the Torah, and as a consequence, the majority of them were deprived of the Quran as well. Here, the Quran warns the hypocrites against this evil approach and its outcome: you too, like the Jews, are suffering from uncertainty in the presence of your Messenger; let not this disease prove fatal for you as it did for them.

(Know that Allah gives life to the earth after its death! Indeed We have made clear the communications to you, that you may understand) (17). (This verse appears in a highly appropriate place here, containing a cure for the uncertainty of these hypocrites from two different angles).

(The cure for uncertainty): The most prominent aspect is that if a person lacks certainty in the Hereafter, sacrificing life and wealth becomes extremely difficult. The primary illness of these hypocrites was their lack of certainty in the Hereafter, which is why they viewed the promises of the Quran as mere wishful thinking. To remove their uncertainty, Allah drew their attention to the signs and proofs He has detailed in His Book showing the Resurrection as an absolute reality. Since the objective here is simply to remind them of the reality of the Hereafter, He pointed to an obvious proof of its possibility and declared that He has explained these signs in detail in the Quran so that they may contemplate them and find the courage to sacrifice life and wealth.

The second aspect is that these hypocrites were highly intimidated by the power of the opponents of Islam. They could not understand how a handful of Muslims could cope with the massive armies of the enemy, and how they could dismantle the established government of Quraysh in Makkah to make the Kabah once again the center of the Abrahamic call. Although some battles had occurred in which the Muslims had the upper hand, fear had not yet left the hearts of the hypocrites. They remained pessimistic about the future of Islam. To remove their despair, Allah directed them to the sign of the earth: do not consider it beyond the power of the God Whom you see reviving the dead earth to revive this land of Arabia with the life of faith and Islam.

(Verily, those who give Sadqaat, men and women, and lend to Allah a goodly loan, it shall be increased manifold for them, and theirs shall be an honorable reward) (18)

This presents the theme of verse 11 from another perspective, encouraging the hypocrites that spending in Allah's way is not a losing transaction but a highly profitable business. Those who spend regularly, and who also stand up generously to help when an appeal is made during an emergency, will find every single penny of theirs preserved with Allah. He is multiplying it for them and will return it to them in the form of an everlasting treasure. In addition, there is a great, honorable reward for them—meaning that what they gave will be returned multiplied several times, and furthermore, the Generous Lord will grant them a great reward from His presence.

(Charity and Loan): Here, the words 'Sadqah' (charity) and 'Qard' (loan) are both used for spending: the first in the form of a noun/attribute, and the second as a verb. In my view, the reason for this variation is that one type of spending is that required of every financially able Muslim under normal circumstances as an essential part of the purification of the soul. The second is the spending required on special occasions for the defense and protection of the community. The first is referred to here by the term 'Sadqah' using noun forms because it is constantly required, while the second is referred to as a 'loan' because it is given when needed, hence the use of the verbal form.

(The wisdom of specifically mentioning women alongside men here): In these verses, it is noteworthy that in describing hypocrisy and spending, the Quran explicitly highlights the responsibility of women alongside men. Reflection reveals the reason: wives and children are major factors in prompting miserliness and cowardice, and these two traits lead directly to hypocrisy. This reality is also stated in the Hadith, where it is mentioned that wives and children can be a cause of miserliness and cowardice. Due to this aspect, 'Munasaddiqeen' is mentioned alongside 'Munasaddiqaat' (and 'Musaddiqeen' alongside 'Musaddiqaat') with special care, so that it becomes clear to women as well as men that everyone will be held accountable for their responsibilities before Allah, whether male or female.

(And those who believe in Allah and His Messengers, they are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord. They shall have their reward and their light. But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs, they shall be the dwellers of the Fire) (19). The context indicates that just as the word 'faith' (Iman) was used in its complete sense in previous verses, it is used in its true and complete sense here as well.

(A warning to the hypocrites): The hypocrites are being told that the ranks of the truthful (Siddiqeen) and the witnesses (Shuhada) with Allah will not be granted to every mere claimant of faith, but are reserved for those who believe truly and firmly in Allah and His Messengers and prove the truth of their claim through their actions. Since spending is the primary topic under discussion, it follows naturally that those who spend their wealth generously to support the faith are the ones who are true in their claim to faith, and they are the ones who will have the honor of being included among the truthful and the witnesses.

(Definition of Siddiq): The analysis of the word 'Siddiq' has been presented in its respective place: its essence lies in the complete consistency and strength between word and deed. In Arabic, a spear is called 'Sadiq al-Ka'b' when its joints prove as strong under trial as they appear to the eye. 'Sadiq' and 'Siddiq' refer to a person who is true to his word. The first practical testimony of this strength is that he is ready to sacrifice his hard-earned wealth for the sake of the truth he has declared. Through this sacrifice, he becomes a witness to the truth, and it is from such a person that we can expect that if a trial arises, he will sacrifice his life as well to bear witness to it. In Islam, the most prominent example of this attribute is Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him). The manner in which he proved the truth of his faith through spending during the most critical times is the brightest chapter of our history.

(Witness/Martyr): 'Shuhada' (witnesses) is used here in the same sense as in the verse: 'Thus We have made you a middle nation, that you be witnesses over mankind and the Messenger be a witness over you' (Surah Al-Baqarah: 143). This means that the great honor of being 'witnesses over mankind' will not be achieved merely by claiming faith; it will be granted only to those whose every limb bears witness to their faith. It should also be clarified that those killed in the way of Allah are called 'Shahid' (martyr) because they sacrificed their lives to fulfill this duty of bearing witness to the truth over mankind. Since this is the greatest form of testimony, they are called martyrs. This very point was addressed to these hypocrites in Surah An-Nisa: 'And whosoever obeys Allah and the Messenger, then they will be in the company of those on whom Allah has bestowed His Grace, of the Prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous' (Surah An-Nisa: 70). (They shall have their reward and their light) means that the reward and light described in verses 11 and 12 are reserved for these devoted individuals; not every claimant of faith is entitled to them. (But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs, they shall be the dwellers of the Fire) means that all those who committed disbelief and denial will enter Hell. The context indicates that this same ruling applies to those who, though they did not deny with their tongues, did not confirm with their deeds, acting in complete contradiction to their claim.

(Know that the life of this world is only play and amusement, pomp and mutual boasting among you, and rivalry in respect of wealth and children, as the likeness of vegetation after rain, thereof the growth is pleasing to the disbelievers; then it dries up and you see it turning yellow; then it becomes straw. But in the Hereafter there is a severe torment, and forgiveness from Allah and His Good Pleasure. And the life of this world is only a deceiving enjoyment) (20).

(A warning to those obsessed with wealth): This is an admonition to those wealth-worshiping hypocrites: the life of this world—consisting of amusement, play, desire for adornment, the race to multiply wealth and children, and competing to raise one's standard of living, in which you are constantly immersed—is not an activity with a good end. Its likeness is that of a rain that causes vegetation to grow, delighting the ungrateful, but then some calamity strikes it so that it dries up, turns yellow, and is reduced to broken pieces. This is the ultimate end of all your efforts to acquire this world and outdo one another; none of it will remain.

(But in the Hereafter there is a severe torment, and forgiveness from Allah and His Good Pleasure. And the life of this world is only a deceiving enjoyment) means that out of your ignorance, you have made the pleasures of this world your life's goal, whereas this world is merely a deceptive enjoyment. The true goal is the Hereafter, where one of two outcomes must be faced: on one side is severe punishment, and on the other is Allah's forgiveness and His pleasure. If a person cannot bring himself to sacrifice worldly desires to achieve Allah's forgiveness and pleasure, he will inevitably face severe punishment.

(A specific point): In this verse, most commentators structure the sentence by taking (the life of this world) as the subject (Mubtada) and the subsequent words as the predicate (Khabar). However, in my view, the words (play and amusement, pomp and mutual boasting among you, and rivalry in respect of wealth and children) serve as an apposition (Badal), and (as the likeness of vegetation after rain) is the predicate of the subject. Examples of this sentence structure are common in the Quran, such as: 'We will drag him by the forelock—a lying, sinful forelock' (Surah Al-Alaq: 15-16). This structure significantly affects the meaning. According to general commentators, the verse means that the life of this world is nothing but play, amusement, pomp, and boasting. In my view, the meaning is: the likeness of the life of this world—meaning its play, amusement, pomp, and boasting—is like... Under the commentators' view, this world and its life are deemed detestable in their entirety, supporting a monastic view of life which the Quran rejects in this very Surah. Under my interpretation, it is not the world in its entirety that is deemed detestable, but only that aspect upon which the disbelievers and hypocrites dote, and which leads them to Hell.

From the perspective of religious philosophy, this world and its life are not a curse in themselves; rather, whether they are a curse or a mercy depends on human behavior. If a person lives within the limits prescribed by Allah, this temporary life guarantees the eternal kingdom of the Hereafter; but if he becomes indifferent to these limits, making the world his deity and losing himself in its pleasures, it becomes an eternal curse for him. This verse warns against this latter aspect.

(Another point): The word 'Kuffar' in this verse is also noteworthy. Commentators have generally translated it as 'farmers' (Zurra'), but this is not convincing. This is because the word is not commonly used in this sense. At most, one can say that its root contains the capacity for this meaning, but this is not sufficient to assign a rare meaning to a word that is used abundantly in the Quran as an established term for a specific concept, especially when no other such example exists in the Quran. In Surah Al-Fath, verse 29: 'then it stands close upon its stalk, delighting the sowers (Zurra') that He may enrage the disbelievers (Kuffar) with them'—both words are used in their respective specific meanings, showing that both are well-known and distinct in their usage. Therefore, I am inclined to view 'Kuffar' here in its primary meaning of 'disbelievers.' Since the purpose of this parable is to highlight the attitude of those who deny the Hereafter, it states that the temporary splendors of this world delight the hearts of the disbelievers, who remain trapped within them and ultimately face the punishment decreed for such people. It should be noted that parables and comparisons sometimes include words meant to point to the people intended by the parable. Keeping this in view is essential to appreciate the beauty of the parable; here, the use of this word points to the people intended by the comparison.

(Race one with another for forgiveness from your Lord, and for a Garden whereof the width is as the width of the heaven and the earth, prepared for those who believe in Allah and His Messengers. That is the Grace of Allah which He bestows on whom He pleases, and Allah is the Owner of Great Grace) (21).

(The true field of competition): The preceding verse explained that the efforts, rivalry, and boasting of the disbelievers are confined to the temporary and desired objects of this world; they lack the courage to look beyond them. However, the objective of the believers should be the forgiveness and pleasure of their Lord, and they should compete to outdo one another to obtain the Garden whose width is like the width of the heaven and the earth, prepared by Allah for those true believers who believe firmly in Him and His Messengers. The word 'width' (Ardh) here refers to vastness and expanse, and 'heaven' (Sama) is generic, meaning the heavens, as expressed in another verse: 'And march forth in the way to forgiveness from your Lord, and for a Garden as wide as the heavens and the earth' (Surah Al-Imran: 133).

(Prepared for those who believe in Allah and His Messengers) means: do not consider it a distant thing; it is already prepared and waiting for those who believe in Allah and His Messengers. As soon as the trial of this world ends, Paradise will be unveiled before them. Do not be surprised that Allah will grant such vast Gardens in return for a few days of good deeds. Allah is the Owner of great grace; He bestows it on whom He wills, and there is no shortage in His possession.

(No calamity befalls on the earth or in yourselves but is inscribed in a Book before We bring it into existence. Verily, that is easy for Allah) (22).

(The decree of destiny is unalterable): This means you must always keep this reality in mind: whatever calamity regarding wealth or life is destined to reach you will inevitably do so. No matter how many efforts you make to avoid it, you cannot escape it. Every joy and sorrow has been recorded beforehand by Allah in a Book, and it is not within anyone's power to change the written decree. Allah's knowledge encompasses both the present and the future; therefore, this task is not difficult for Him in the least. You may consider it beyond possibility based on your own limited knowledge, but Allah is the Creator of all things. It is a simple matter for Him to record the destiny of what He brings into existence before He actually creates it.

Explaining this point in the context of the invitation to spend is necessary because those who withhold their wealth and lives from the way of Allah do so under the misconception that they acquired their wealth through their own planning and that they are the protectors of their own lives. To remove this misconception, He stated that wealth is a gift from Allah; thus, attempting to hide it from Him is absolute foolishness. How can anyone hide what was received through His favor? If a person behaves miserly regarding Allah's right, Allah can send a calamity to take his wealth away whenever He wills. The correct approach for a human being is to fulfill his duty and hope that just as his Lord helped him today, He will help him in every future difficulty. In other places, the Quran describes the character of the hypocrites and misers as withholding their spending out of fear of what might happen tomorrow. They view their miserliness as a cure for future difficulties, whereas whatever is destined to happen is unalterable. The remedy is not miserliness, but spending in His way and placing trust in Him.

(On the earth) points to those calamities affecting agricultural produce and wealth, while (in yourselves) refers to illnesses and afflictions affecting the body and life. The trials that man faces in this world occur through these two avenues. It is stated that whatever is to occur through these avenues is already written with Allah. Therefore, it is not permissible for man to withhold his wealth or his life from Him, for he cannot protect either of them from Him. Addressing these same hypocrites elsewhere, the Quran states that today you turn away from Allah's way and fear death, but the appointed time of death will arrive, and it will find you even if you hide inside fortified towers.

(In a Book) refers to the Book in which Allah has recorded the destiny of every person, also referred to as the Preserved Tablet (Lawh al-Mahfuz), which represents Divine Knowledge. (Verily, that is easy for Allah) means that your knowledge is limited, which is why it is difficult for you to conceive that everything that happens in an individual's life is recorded, but for Allah this task is not difficult at all. He is the Creator of this entire universe, and His knowledge encompasses all; He knows the stages through which each individual will pass to reach their final destination.

(In order that you may not grieve at the things over which you fail, nor rejoice over that which has been given to you. And Allah likes not prideful boasters) (23).

(The philosophy of destiny): The sentence structure implies a missing element before 'in order that' (likayla). If we fill this in, the point is: you are being reminded of this reality so that you do not grieve over what you lose, nor boast and exult over what is given to you. In the light of this belief, your character should be such that if you suffer a physical or financial loss, you observe patience, knowing that it occurred in accordance with the decree of destiny and contains Allah's wisdom. If you receive a benefit, you should be grateful to your Lord, knowing that He blessed you out of His grace. Do not fall into the delusion of arrogance, thinking that it is the result of your own planning and ability.

(And Allah likes not prideful boasters): Those who become arrogant upon receiving Allah's blessings must remember that Allah does not love any arrogant, boasting person. Although the words 'does not love' (la yuhibbu) appear soft, the meaning is severe. As we have explained elsewhere, when such a negative style is used, the positive equivalent would be: 'Verily, Allah detests every arrogant boaster.' However, the speaker expresses the intensity of detestation through tone and style rather than using explicit words of anger.

(Those who are miserly and enjoin miserliness upon people. And whosoever turns away, then Allah is the Rich, the Worthy of all praise) (24)

(This points to another aspect of their character): Since such people are under the delusion that whatever they acquire is due to their own planning, they view spending in the way of religion and community as a loss and a fine, preferring to spend on show, adornment, and boasting. They practice miserliness themselves and advise others to be miserly as well, so that their own miserliness remains concealed. The Quran highlights this aspect of misers in several places: they wish that others remain as miserly as they are so that their own conduct is not exposed.

The word 'enjoin' (amr) is used here in the sense of 'advising.' This usage is well-known. This means that when they see others spending on religious and communal works, they offer them 'sincere' advice, saying: 'Do not be so extravagant; if a difficult time comes, you will regret it, and no one will help you.' (And whosoever turns away, then Allah is the Rich, the Worthy of all praise) is an expression of complete detachment and warning to such misers: if they insist on turning away after this admonition, they must remember that Allah is entirely independent of them. He invites them to spend not because of any shortage in His treasures, but to open a path of eternal profit for His servants (as stated in verse 18). The attribute 'Al-Hameed' (Worthy of praise) alongside 'Al-Ghani' (Rich) expresses the reality that Allah is inherently independent and praiseworthy. He requires nothing from anyone; when He asks of people, it is not because He is in need of them, but because He is 'Hameed' and wishes to make them deserving of His favors, transforming their worthless worldly items into eternal treasures before returning them.

6. Subject Matter of Verses 25–29

These are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which an objection raised by the People of the Book—particularly the Christians—is addressed. The spending to which the believers were invited in the preceding verses is related to Jihad, as is clear from the context. As long as the Muslims remained oppressed and dominated in Makkah, the People of the Book and the Quraysh used their weakness as an argument against them. However, when they formed an organized community in Madinah and defeated the Quraysh in battles despite their small numbers, both groups began to propagate against their spirit of Jihad, saying: 'How can this be a religious group when they consider fighting and bloodshed to be the greatest virtue? The messengers and righteous servants of Allah have nothing to do with fighting; they are callers to peace, safety, and love!' Since a monastic view of religion dominated minds during that era, the Quraysh adopted this objection raised by the People of the Book. During the Battle of Badr, they taunted the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), saying: 'How can he be a Messenger of Allah when he has caused brother's sword to fall upon brother's neck?' The Quran addresses this objection in several places, including Surah Al-Anfal and Surah Al-Hajj. Here, in response, three points are clarified in the light of the history of the Prophets:

First, Allah sent His Messengers so that people might walk upon the path of truth and justice in the light of their guidance. For this purpose, He revealed His Book to serve as a scale of truth (Meezan), enabling them to distinguish between truth and falsehood, justice and oppression.

Second, establishing justice requires preventing oppression. This necessitates that power and strength accompany the Book and the scale, so that any opposing forces attempting to obstruct justice can be subdued. For this purpose, Allah revealed iron alongside the Book, which contains severe power along with other benefits for humanity, providing the strength necessary for Jihad to establish truth and justice. This Jihad is a trial through which Allah distinguishes the sincere from the hypocrites, revealing those who are ready to sacrifice their lives for the truth.

Third, all the Prophets who came from the progeny of Noah and Abraham (peace be upon them) came for this purpose of establishing justice, and Allah sent Jesus, son of Mary (peace be upon him), upon their path. Allah placed compassion and mercy in the hearts of his followers, which later generations used as a pretext to invent monasticism (Rahbaniyyah)—an innovation of their own. What was ordained for them was only to seek Allah's pleasure, which is the common teaching of all Prophets, but due to their exaggeration, they transgressed the limits and ruined the form of the religion by inventing monasticism. Now, they use this distorted religion as an argument to object to the command of Jihad in Islam.

Since the Christians suffered the greatest deviation due to this fitnah of monasticism, they are explicitly invited to Islam at the end: those who claim belief in Jesus should not remain deprived of the great blessing of Islam by remaining entangled in innovations like monasticism. If they believe in the final Messenger, Allah will grant them a double portion of His mercy as promised, while the Jews, who consider themselves the sole monopolists of Allah's grace, will continue to burn in the fire of their jealousy. With this perspective, recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

(Indeed We have sent Our Messengers with clear proofs, and revealed with them the Scripture and the Balance that mankind may keep up justice. And We brought down iron wherein is mighty power, as well as many benefits for mankind, that Allah may test who helps Him and His Messengers in the unseen. Verily, Allah is All-Strong, All-Mighty) (25).

(The purpose of sending Messengers): The purpose of sending Messengers and revealing Books is clarified: Allah sent His Messengers and revealed Books so that people might adopt truth and justice in every aspect of life. In beliefs, they should adopt the straight, middle path free from any deviations; and in actions—whether related to individual or collective life—they should follow the way of justice, free from any trace of oppression. This shows that Allah did not send His Messengers merely to deliver sermons, nor did He reveal His Books merely for recitation; rather, the actual purpose was that people establish and maintain justice under their guidance.

In my view, mentioning the 'Balance' (Meezan) alongside the 'Book' serves to clarify the greatest purpose of the Book: it weighs and reveals how much truth a person possesses and what unwarranted additions have been made. This aspect of the Book is explained clearly in Surah Ash-Shura. There, it is first clarified that all Messengers taught only monotheism (Tawheed) and strictly forbade any deviation from it. Afterward, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed to invite people to this path and inform his opponents: 'I believe in the Book that Allah has revealed; how then can I follow your innovations? Allah has appointed me to judge between you and to weigh with this scale to show how much truth and falsehood each of you possesses.' This discussion concludes with the verse: 'It is Allah Who has sent down the Book in truth and the Balance. And what can make you know that perhaps the Hour is close at hand?' (Surah Ash-Shura: 17). For this reason, the Quran is also called 'Muhaymin' (the criterion/touchstone)—meaning it is a touchstone used to distinguish the genuine from the counterfeit. Since establishing justice requires a balance and a touchstone, Allah highlighted both of these attributes of the Quran.

(The need for power to establish justice): (And We brought down iron wherein is mighty power, as well as many benefits for mankind) means that since the actual purpose of sending Messengers and Books is to establish justice, this task cannot be achieved solely through sermons, warnings, and glad tidings; it requires power. Therefore, Allah sent His Messengers with clear proofs and made the Books a balance and touchstone to establish intellectual and moral proof, and He also revealed iron to subdue those who refuse to submit to the truth and insist on causing corruption in the earth. It has always been the Divine Law that when He sends a Messenger, one of two outcomes must follow after the establishment of proof: if the believers are very few and the majority remains in disbelief, Allah destroys the disbelievers through an earthly or heavenly punishment, saving His believing servants; if the believers are numerous, the Messenger and his companions are commanded to perform physical Jihad, resulting in the elimination of the disbelievers. The history of the Prophets described in the Quran bears witness to this Divine Law.

(Every resource originates from Allah): Although iron is extracted from the earth, the word 'revealed' (anzalna) is used for it, just as it is used for the creation of cattle in the Quran. This is meant, on one hand, to draw attention to the special care for human welfare inherent in it, and on the other hand, to point to the ultimate source of everything—showing that no matter where a thing is obtained, it is ultimately revealed by Allah. Unless a human being views things from this perspective, they cannot appreciate the true value of Allah's blessings.

(A point): In this phrase, 'mighty power' (ba's shadeed) precedes 'benefits' (manafi'), indicating that in the sight of Allah, the primary utility of iron is to provide the strength for Jihad; its other constructive and civilizational benefits are secondary. It should also be noted that the Quran mentions iron here as the primary instrument of warfare of that era, when most weapons were made of it. In modern times, while other materials have gained primary importance, the same principle applies to those materials as applied to iron.

(The wisdom of Jihad): (That Allah may test who helps Him and His Messengers in the unseen. Verily, Allah is All-Strong, All-Mighty) explains the wisdom of Jihad: although Allah is Himself Strong and Dominant, and can defeat His enemies in the blink of an eye, He uses Jihad to test His servants to see who supports Him and His Messengers in the unseen, and who proves insincere under trial. We have explained that 'to know' (li-ya'lama) here means 'to distinguish'; Allah knows all things beforehand, but through such trials, He makes the distinction between the true and the false manifest.

(And indeed, We sent Noah and Abraham, and placed in their offspring Prophethood and Scripture. And among them there is he who is guided, but many of them are rebellious) (26).

(Reference to the history of the Prophets): This refers to the history of all the Prophets: Allah sent them to invite people to establish justice, but only a few among their offspring remained firm on this path, while the majority turned away and became rebellious due to their innovations. Only Noah and Abraham (peace be upon them) are mentioned by name here because Noah is like a second Adam, and Abraham is the patriarch of the Prophets of Israel and Ishmael. Mentioning these two represents the entire chain of Prophethood. This verse serves as an introduction to the theme that follows.

(Then, We sent after them, Our Messengers, and We sent Jesus, son of Mary, and gave him the Gospel. And We ordained in the hearts of those who followed him, compassion and mercy. But the monasticism which they invented for themselves, We did not ordain for them, but only seeking the pleasure of Allah; but they did not observe it with the right observance. So We gave those among them who believed, their reward, but many of them are rebellious) (27).

(This explains how the innovation of monasticism entered the religion): Since the way of all the Prophets was the same as described above, where did the innovation of monasticism and the idea that Jihad is contrary to piety come from? It is stated that after Noah and Abraham, We sent Our Messengers upon their footsteps—meaning all of them invited people to justice, and when necessary, they also performed Jihad in accordance with the way of the Prophets. None of the Messengers acted contrary to this way. Jesus, son of Mary (peace be upon him), was sent for this very purpose and was given the Gospel. He was not sent to teach monasticism. Rather, Allah had placed compassion and mercy in the hearts of his early followers, which later innovators used as a pretext to establish a system of monasticism, though We did not ordain monasticism for them, but only seeking the pleasure of Allah. However, they did not observe its limits, but transgressed the boundaries of nature and Shariah, wandering into entirely different valleys. This clarifies that while the innovation of monasticism entered through the Christians, it was not part of the teaching of Jesus, the Gospel, or his true disciples, but was entirely the invention of later innovators.

(And We ordained in the hearts of those who followed him, compassion and mercy): This praises the early followers of Jesus, stating that Allah placed compassion and mercy in their hearts. The purpose of this praise is to show how these noble human qualities became a pretext for monasticism for later generations, even though compassion and mercy are taught by every Prophet. The Quran describes the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) as: 'eager for your guidance, kind and merciful to the believers' (Surah At-Tawbah: 128). Regarding the Muslims, the phrase used is: 'merciful among themselves' (Surah Al-Fath: 29). The Hadith descriptions are even clearer, stating that the believers are like a single body in their mutual sympathy and compassion.

Compassion and mercy are not contrary to justice; rather, the primary demand of mercy is to establish justice. For this reason, Allah described the Day of Judgment—the day of absolute justice—as a necessary result of His mercy: 'He has prescribed mercy upon Himself. He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection' (Surah An-An'am: 12). Since the followers of Jesus did not find the opportunity for Jihad during his life, later generations used their compassion as an argument for monasticism. Had they found the opportunity for Jihad, as did the Ummah of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), they too would have embodied the description: 'strong against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves.'

(Monasticism is an innovation): (But the monasticism which they invented for themselves, We did not ordain for them, but only seeking the pleasure of Allah; but they did not observe it with the right observance) means that while We placed compassion and mercy in their hearts, this monasticism which they invented had nothing to do with the teachings of Jesus. 'Monasticism' (Rahbaniyyan) is not governed by 'We ordained' (Ja'alna); its grammatical accusative case is governed by an implied verb clarified by the subsequent verb 'they invented' (Ibtada'uha). However, its mention here shows that while monasticism originated from a good motive, it constituted a transgression of the limits prescribed by Allah, leading its practitioners into error due to exaggeration and extremism.

(We did not ordain for them, but only seeking the pleasure of Allah) means that this monasticism was not ordained by Us; We only ordained for them the struggle to seek Allah's pleasure. Had they confined themselves to this, it would have been a source of goodness for them in this world and the next, but they did not observe its limits. (But they did not observe it with the right observance): The feminine pronoun refers to 'monasticism' (Rahbaniyyah), indicating that the primary error of the Christians lay in failing to observe its limits, which allowed unnatural and irrational elements to enter it. Had they observed its limits, it would not have transgressed the objective of seeking Allah's pleasure—an objective common to all Prophets and scriptures—and Jihad in the way of Allah is a major part of this pursuit, as clarified in Surah At-Tawbah: 'Indeed, Allah has purchased of the believers their lives and their properties for Paradise. They fight in His cause, so they kill and are killed. It is a true promise binding upon Him in the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran' (Surah At-Tawbah: 111).

This shows that fighting to seek Allah's pleasure is a common teaching of all Prophets and scriptures, and was also taught to the Christians in the Gospel, which emphasized adherence to the laws of the Torah. However, they invented a form of monasticism in which this original objective was lost.

Recall what was clarified under the term 'Sa'ihoon' in Surah At-Tawbah: we find both positive and negative statements regarding monasticism and travel. This shows that these practices are not blameworthy in themselves; rather, the defect arises when their limits are not preserved, as indicated by the words: 'but they did not observe it with the right observance.' Thus, Islam incorporated all elements of monasticism and travel that are in harmony with human nature and the pursuit of Allah's pleasure, while excluding those unnatural elements invented by innovators. Reflecting on the Islamic system of worship—including prayer, fasting, pilgrimage, and spending—reveals that every act contains healthy elements of monasticism. In some Hadiths, Jihad is even referred to as the monasticism of Islam.

(Praise of the true Christians): (So We gave those among them who believed, their reward, but many of them are rebellious): The context indicates that 'believed' (amanu) is used in its complete sense, referring to those followers of Jesus who remained firm upon his true teachings, receiving their reward, while the majority turned away. 'Those who believed' refers to the true disciples of Jesus, like Simon, and those who followed in their footsteps, remaining on the true religion despite all difficulties. When the call of Islam arose, these righteous remnants welcomed it with sincere eagerness, and the Quran praises their truthfulness in several places. (But many of them are rebellious) refers to Paul and his followers, who distorted the Christian religion by inventing the Trinity and monasticism, incorporating Roman pagan practices into their faith. Eventually, this group of innovators became the majority, leaving very few who knew and adhered to the original teachings of Jesus.

AyahArabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَآمَنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ
Tafseer:
(O you who believe! Fear Allah, and believe in His Messenger, He will give you a double portion of His Mercy, and He will give you a light by which you shall walk, and He will forgive you. And Allah is Oft-Forgiving, Most Merciful) (28).

(Inviting the true Christians to faith): Here, 'O you who believe' addresses those Christians mentioned in the preceding verse as 'We gave those among them who believed, their reward.' Following their mention, those Christians who still possessed some faith are invited to believe in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). They are addressed: O you who believe (i.e., in Jesus), fear Allah and believe in His Messenger (Muhammad), and He will grant you a double portion of His mercy, and will provide for you a manifest light in which you will walk in the Hereafter, and will forgive you, for He is Forgiving and Merciful. In Surah Al-Ma'idah, a group of Christians is praised in these words: 'And you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, "We are Christians." That is because among them are priests and monks and because they are not arrogant. And when they hear what has been revealed to the Messenger, you see their eyes overflowing with tears because of what they have recognized of the truth. They say, "Our Lord, we have believed, so register us among the witnesses"' (Surah Al-Ma'idah: 82-83).

In this verse, all Christians who possess any remnant of their previous faith are invited to believe in the unlettered Prophet: fear Allah and believe in this Messenger whom you were commanded to follow. The warning to fear Allah is given because many Christians recognized that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was indeed the unlettered Prophet foretold in the Gospel, but they feared those who opposed Islam, worrying that acknowledging this truth would turn people into their enemies. Because of this fear, they are warned: do not fear people, but fear Allah alone; if you ignore the fear of Allah out of fear of people, consider the consequences carefully.

(The promise of a double reward): (He will give you a double portion of His Mercy) reiterates the promise made to the righteous among the People of the Book in Surah Al-Qasas. It is stated there that those among the People of the Book who remained firm on their religion, and then believed in the final Messenger when he came in accordance with the prophecies of their scriptures, will receive a double reward. This is because they had to pass through two difficult trials to bear witness to the truth, and they succeeded in both: 'Those to whom We gave the Scripture before it—they are believers in it. And when it is recited to them, they say, "We have believed in it; indeed, it is the truth from our Lord. Indeed we were, before this, Muslims." Those will be given their reward twice for what they patiently endured and [because] they prevent evil with good, and from what We have provided them they spend' (Surah Al-Qasas: 52-54).

As explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Qasas, 'those to whom We gave the Scripture' refers to the righteous among the People of the Book—particularly the Christians—whose love of truth and fear of the Lord are mentioned in Surah Al-Ma'idah.

It should be clarified that the righteous among the People of the Book receive this double reward not merely because they are from the People of the Book, but because they bore witness to the truth revealed to Jesus and previous Prophets, and also bore witness to the truth revealed to the unlettered Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Since this Ummah bears witness to all the truth revealed by Allah without any distinction or sectarianism, whether revealed to previous Prophets or to the Prophet Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it is entitled to this double reward. Thus, anyone who enters this Ummah will share in this honor. This is clarified in the Hadiths, and since there is no disagreement on this issue, there is no need for further elaboration.

(And He will give you a light by which you shall walk, and He will forgive you): This is a glad tidings similar to the one mentioned in verse 12 of this Surah: 'On the Day you shall see the believing men and the believing women: their light running forward before them and by their right hands.' The same glad tidings is given to these righteous individuals: they protected the light of faith that Allah gave them, guided themselves by it, and guided others. In return, they will receive a light in the Hereafter to walk through the darkness of that Day, and others who supported them in the world will seek to benefit from their light.

(Lest the People of the Book should know that they have no power over any of the Grace of Allah, and that the Grace is in the Hand of Allah to give it to whom He wills. And Allah is the Owner of Great Grace) (29).

(Reference to the jealousy of the Jews): This encourages the believers to strive to achieve this great grace and to ignore the jealous Jews, leaving them to burn in the fire of their jealousy against the unlettered Prophet and his followers. The cause of their jealousy is their ignorance in assuming that they hold the monopoly over all of Allah's grace. They do not realize that they are not the owners of His grace; rather, Allah Himself is the Owner of His grace, bestowing it on whom He wills, and He is the Owner of great grace.

(The People of the Book): Although the term is general and can refer to both Jews and Christians, the context indicates that here it refers specifically to the Jews as a group. This is because they were the ones who suffered from the delusion of being the sole heirs and monopolists of Allah's grace and Prophethood. They assumed that no one outside their ranks could receive Prophethood, and that no one else was entitled to reward or grace from Allah. Due to this delusion, they fiercely opposed the Prophet's claim to Prophethood. When they saw that despite their opposition, this call continued to spread—even drawing many of their own noble individuals—they were consumed by such intense jealousy that it ultimately led to their ruin. The causes and consequences of this jealousy are described in detail in Surah Al-Baqarah.

The phrase (Lest the People of the Book should know...) points to the inevitable result of their ignorance—namely, their persistent jealousy. They were consumed by this jealousy because they viewed themselves as the ancestral heirs to all of Allah's grace. Had they not been trapped in this foolishness, but realized that Prophethood, leadership, and grace are gifts from Allah which He bestows on whom He wills, they would have had no cause for anger or jealousy. They would have understood that just as Allah honored them with this position in the past, He has now chosen someone else, and no one can oppose His decree; thus, their well-being lay in accepting their Lord's decision. However, they persisted in their delusion, which only fueled their anger and jealousy. The point stated alongside the invitation to believe in the final Messenger is that the believers should secure their double reward by believing, and leave these jealous Jews to burn in their jealousy.

This style of expression allowed the root cause of the Jews' illness to be clarified without introducing bitterness into the discourse. Our commentators have faced difficulty in interpreting these concluding verses of the Surah. Had we cited and critiqued their views, it would have led to great length without much benefit. Therefore, we have presented the interpretation we believe to be correct in the light of the language, structure, and precedents of the Quran. We hope this provides clarity to those who reflect on it. Note two points regarding the language and style:

First, in Arabic, the focus of a discourse is sometimes not on the literal words, but on the ultimate result. For example, the phrase 'Lest the People of the Book should know that they have no power over any of the Grace of Allah' actually points to the result—namely, that they remain consumed by the jealousy resulting from their ignorance.

Second, the particle 'la' (not) here is neither redundant nor is there any redundant particle in the Quran. As explained in its respective place, it is not permissible to assume redundant particles in the Quran, as this would open the door for innovators to introduce false interpretations into the religion. However, for emphasis and clarity, it is sometimes repeated in accordance with the established rules of the Arabic language, with several examples existing in the Quran and classical Arabic discourse, such as: 'What prevented you that you did not prostrate' (Surah Al-A'raf: 12) or 'And a ban is laid on any town which We have destroyed that they shall not return' (Surah Al-Anbiya: 95).

With the help of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is complete. All praise is due to Allah for this. Rahmanabad. December 15, 1977.
